coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent metal doped carbon quantum dots prepared from hen feather demonstrating pH-dependent dual sensing of 4-nitrophenol and Hg2+ ion
coredata.dc:creator: Das, Sohel
Sankar Mondal, Uma
Paul, Subhankar
coredata.dc:description: Mg/Zn modified carbon quantum dots (CQD) were synthesized from hen feather for the fluorescence-based pH dependent optical sensing of mercury (Hg2+)and p-Nitrophenol (4-NP). TEMimaging confirmed the size of QDs as ∼ 3.4 nm for Mg doped CQDs (MgCQD) and ∼ 4.09 nm for Zn doped CQDs (ZnCQD). ZnCQD showed the lowest band gap, and highest fluorescence quantum yield (QY) compared to native CQD and MgCQD. MgCQD and ZnCQD demonstrated 70% and 52% fluorescence quenching, respectively, in the presence of 20 ppm of Hg2+ at pH 7. For0–20 ppm Hg2+ concentration range, two linear detection regions were observed by both the QDs. Further, the prepared QDs also showed high selectivity towards 4-NP at pH 2. The detection of 4-NP was highly linear for both MgCQD and ZnCQD. The fluorescence of MgCQD and ZnCQD was found quenched more than 90% in the presence of 20 ppm 4-NP in acidic pH. Therefore, our synthesized metal-doped QDs prepared fromhen feather wastedemonstrated efficient pH-responsive dual sensing of Hg2+ and 4-NP and are alsovery cheap, rapid, and can be used for multifunctional pollutant detection in the aquatic environment.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent metal doped carbon quantum dots prepared from hen feather demonstrating pH-dependent dual sensing of 4-nitrophenol and Hg2+ ion

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biotechnology and Medical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mg/Zn modified carbon quantum dots (CQD) were synthesized from hen feather for the fluorescence-based pH dependent optical sensing of mercury (Hg2+)and p-Nitrophenol (4-NP). TEMimaging confirmed the size of QDs as ∼ 3.4 nm for Mg doped CQDs (MgCQD) and ∼ 4.09 nm for Zn doped CQDs (ZnCQD). ZnCQD showed the lowest band gap, and highest fluorescence quantum yield (QY) compared to native CQD and MgCQD. MgCQD and ZnCQD demonstrated 70% and 52% fluorescence quenching, respectively, in the presence of 20 ppm of Hg2+ at pH 7. For0–20 ppm Hg2+ concentration range, two linear detection regions were observed by both the QDs. Further, the prepared QDs also showed high selectivity towards 4-NP at pH 2. The detection of 4-NP was highly linear for both MgCQD and ZnCQD. The fluorescence of MgCQD and ZnCQD was found quenched more than 90% in the presence of 20 ppm 4-NP in acidic pH. Therefore, our synthesized metal-doped QDs prepared fromhen feather wastedemonstrated efficient pH-responsive dual sensing of Hg2+ and 4-NP and are alsovery cheap, rapid, and can be used for multifunctional pollutant detection in the aquatic environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last few decades, exponential industrial growth and poor quality of waste management have caused severe chemical contamination in the aquatic environment due to the disposal of toxic heavy metals, dyes, pharmaceutical reagents, and pesticides in water from textile, paper, petroleum, pharmaceutical, heavy and electronic industries [1–4,99–101]. These activities deteriorate the ecosystem, affect human health and create water scarcity. Therefore, finding strategies for the early detection of these contaminants currently is of great demand. Among different aquatic contaminants, mercury (Hg2+) and p-Nitrophenol (4-NP) have been considered to be highly hazardous to human health, especially, for the central nervous system even at low concentrations [5]. Environmental protection agency has also registered these two contaminants in its priority control list [6]. Therefore, the development of low-cost early detection strategies for Hg2+ and 4-NP using a single sensing platform is currently considered to be of high priority and significance.

So far, plenty of methods have been thoroughly investigated and further commercialized for Hg2+ and 4-NP detection, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [7], liquid chromatography [8,9], electro-voltammetry [10], phosphorimetric method [11], UV–Vis spectrophotometry [12] and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry [13]. Although these methods showed to be highly sensitive and effective, they are associated with complex synthesis procedures, low detection limits, high time consumption, costly materials, higher use of sensor materials, and restrictions for online monitoring systems [14]. In contrast, recently, quantum dot (QD) based fluorescent probes have attracted much attention, due to their fast, sensitive, selective, easy-to-operate, and low-cost detection strategies compared to conjugated polymers [15,16] and organic chromophores [17].

In recent years, among commonly investigated QDs, especially compared to inorganic semiconductor QDs such as CdSe [18,19], CdTe [20], HgTe [21], ZnS [22], ZnSe [23], CdS [24]. etc. Carbon quantum dot (CQD) has gained significant importance for its excellent photo-luminescent and chemical properties, demonstrating lower toxicity, and higher biocompatibility [25]. It was found in various applications, such as in optical sensor design [26], bio-imaging [27,28], drug delivery [29], photocatalysis [30], and energy conversion devices [31]. Although CQD demonstrated good optical and electronic properties, it shows a lack of high fluorescence quantum yield (QY). Also, sometimes these quantum dots struggle through poor water and optical stability, which limits their practical applications [32]. Therefore, recent interest was further focused on heteroatom doping or the use of different precursors for the preparation of CQD, towards the improvement of its QY and functionalities [33].

Heteroatom doping is involved in injecting new electrons in the valence band of the CQD, thus introducing new intermediate electronic states, improving the band gap of the QD and its QY, and demonstrating novel functionalities [32,34]. It also enhances the crystalline nature and compactness of the carbon network [35] and offers more active sites. Normally, during the growth of the CQD, the graphitic cores self-assemble forming aggregates via the π–π stacking effect, however, due to doping this stacking is prevented [36], providing compact and crystalline nature to the QDs [37]. This also provides both sp2 and sp3 nature to the QDs. The most thoroughly investigated doping elements were found to be nitrogen [38,39], phosphorus [40], boron [41], Si [42], and sulphur [43]; whereas very few studies on metal ion doped CQDs, such as Cu2+ [44,45], Zn2+ [36,46], Co2+ [47], Fe3+ [48], Mn2+ [49], Mg2+ [50], Cr3+ [51] were found. It was also observed that most of the CQDs were synthesized from graphite or natural carbon sources including polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon, citric acid [52], ascorbic acid [53], glucose [54], sucrose, fructose, different plants [55,97,98] and biopolymers [56]. Since nature contains abundant sources of carbon, investigations have also been carried out to extract CQDs from different waste sources [57–60], which are low in biodegradability or hard to remove from nature and can provide new different functionalities to the CQDs [61]. However, the synthesis of metal doped CQDs prepared from animal wastes were less explored [62]. As the functionality of the QDs could improve using different precursor, and could also provide a solution for conversion of waste to a value added product, thus CQDs produced from low biodegradable animal waste is currently a hot topic. Xu et al. demonstrated Zn doped CQD for the detection of mercury, where the QD was produced from sodium citrate [36]. In another literature, Xue et al. demonstrated CQD prepared from coal for the detection of phenol [32] . Simialrly, in [63], authors have demonstrated the detection of both Cr and 4-NP using N-doped CQD, prepared from ethylene glycol and β-alanine. It was observed, although few work has been carried out in various doped CQD and using it for the sensing of mercury and 4-NP, they all lack in an aspect of multiple sensing and being produced from a waste source. Moreover, as the mechanism behind metal doped CQD and their enhanced optoelectronic properties still remain contradictory, hence, more explorations are needed in this field.

Here, we attempted to recycle animal waste, hen feathers, to produce Mg doped CQDs (MgCQD) and Zn doped CQDs (ZnCQD). We further applied both the QDs to develop fluorescence (PL) based pH-dependent mercury (Hg2+) and 4-NP sensing strategy in an aqueous solution. This work is the first of its kind for pH-dependent dual sensing of mercury and 4-NP in an aqueous solution. We characterized our QDs through transmission electron microscopy imaging (TEM), Raman spectroscopy and FTIR spectroscopy for their structural and surface analysis. We further evaluated the optical efficiency of our prepared QDs in different pH and found that our prepared QDs work more efficiently for mercury sensing at pH 7 and for a 4-NP sensing in acidic pH with a concentration range of 0–20 ppm. The data trend of the fluorescence vs concentration curve was fitted with different non-linear and linear modelling to find the best-fitted model.

Hen feather was collected from local meat market (Rourkela, Odisha), Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2, 6 H2O), Magnesium sulphate (MgSO4) and Sodium Hydroxide pellets were purchased from HiMedia. Sodium pyrophosphates (Na4P2O7), and sodium fluoride (NaF) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich. All the phenol derivatives (Phenol, 4-nitrophenol, tri-nitrophenol, di-nitrophenol, 2-nitrophenol) were purchased from Loba-chemie. Milli-Q water was used in all experiments. All other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further treatment.

CQD was synthesized from hen feather wastes, collected from the local meat market. The feather is rich in fibrous proteins such as keratin. Initially, the feathers were thoroughly washed to remove the dirt and dried at room temperature, followed by cutting into smaller pieces for easy use. Initially, the process parameters for the CQD synthesis were optimized based on carbonization temperature, sonication and microwave time (Table S1). After multiple trials, all the paramters were fixed based on the solubility, PL property and size of the QD. The dry feathers were heated in a muffle furnace for 3.5 h at 350 °C to break the sulphide bonds, carbonize the keratin structure and form cyclic amines and aromatic carbons [64]. The carbon extracted from the feather (CEF) was weighed. The CEF was grinded to make it a more fine powder through mortar -pestle. Then in 200 mL of milli-Q water, 20 mg of CEF powder was added, the pH was increased to 11 and then it was sonicated for 5 min of 4 cycles with 2 min of a gap in between. The sonicated solution was microwaved at 900 W for 6 min to obtain highly blue fluorescent CQDs. The volume of the solution was reduced to around 100 mL due to evaporation, and it was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 6 min to settle down the larger particles and the solution was stored at a cold temperature. To extract the CQDs as solid particles the initial solution was filtered to remove impurities and bigger particles and then it was freeze-dried for 24 hrs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Mg and Zn doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of ZnCQD and MgCQD, (Zn(NO3)2, 6 H2O) and (MgSO4), respectively, were used. After the sonication of grinded CEF, salts with 10% wt. of CEF used were slowly added to a 200 mL solution. The solution was similarly treated through a microwave system under 900 W and 6 min. This helped the metallic ions to coordinate with carbon atoms and further shrink down the particle size. After the microwave synthesis the solution was centrifuged to remove the larger particles and the brown-colored solution was freeze-dried to extract the doped- CQD.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Jeol, Japan) imaging was performed to observe the structural property of the prepared QDs. The sample was suspended in water and deposited on a copper grid (300 mesh) by the drop casting method. The sample was dried under vacuum conditions for 24 h before performing the TEM analysis. The band gap of QDs was analyzed by a spectrophotometer (λ-35, Perkin Elmer Pvt. Ltd.). The absorption spectra were measured in the range of 200–800 nm, with a slit width of 2 nm and a scanning rate of 100 nm/min. All the samples were diluted to get absorption and normalized the spectra to compare peak maxima. The energy of absorption wavelength was also calculated. Simultaneously, the stability of QD was determined by zeta potential analysis using a zeta sizer (Nano-ZS, Malvern Pvt. Ltd). The samples were prepared in Milli-Q water (filtered using 0.22 μm membrane filter) and investigated by using dynamic light scattering (laser light (λ is 633 nm), at 90° scattering). The data was analyzed using DTS 7.0 software provided by the company. Raman spectroscopy was performed using Witec Alpha300 Confocal Raman microscope, excited at 532 nm. FTIR was carried out using Perkin Elmer FTIR spectrophotometer. The study was carried out in the spectral range of 500–4000 cm−1. All the fluorescence-based experiments were performed using LS-35, Perkin Elmer spectrofluorimeter. Time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy was performed using DeltaFlex lifetime fluorimeter with MaiTai Laser and HPPD detector by Horiba in the emission range of 200–800 nm. A laser pulse of 405 nm wavelength with a pulse repetition of 80 MHz was used for the excitation. The corresponding PL decay curve was fitted using SymPhoTime 64 software. XPS analysis was performed on powder form of the preapare nanomaterials using Physical Electronics, PHI 5000 Versa, Probe III, XPS analyzer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band gap and quantum yield determination
ce:section.ce:para:
The band gap of all the prepared QDs was calculated from their absorbance spectra and their corresponding Tauc plot. Further, the quantum yield (QY) of different CQD was calculated through a relative method, where Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 54% at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm) was used as a standard sample. The concentration of the QD sample was adjusted in such a way as to obtain an absorbance below 0.1 to minimize the inner filter effect. Then the QY of the metal doped CQD was calculated using the following mathematical equation:

Where QY is the quantum yield of the sample, E is the measured sample’s integrated emission intensity, A is the optical density, and n is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript ‘‘s’’ refers to the standard with a known QY (0.546) and the subscript “dots” stands for the CQD samples.

For different pH, NaOH (1.0 M) and HCl (1.0 M) solution were used for the preparation of different pH solution. The study was performed under varying pH from 2 to 12. For each study, 30 µg/mL of CQD (MgCQD, ZnCQD) was used in 4 mL of various pH solutions. After equilibration of 3 min, the fluorescence emission spectra of the solution were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heavy metal and phenol sample preparation and detection using QD
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the prepared material was tested under different ionic conditions using the solution of different salts, such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+ metal ions and phenol derivatives, such as p-nitrophenol (4-NP), picric acid (TNP), di-nitrophenol (DNP) and o-phenol (2-NP). Each sample was first prepared with a stock concentration of 2 mg/mL (2000 ppm). QD stock concentration was kept as 0.2 mg/mL. Before each fluorescence quenching experiment, the mixture of QD and salts was kept in incubation for 1 min. The fluorescence emission spectra were taken multiple times, and it was found that 30 s were enough as an incubation period for obtaining stable fluorescent data. Initially, to test the sensitivity on specific metal ions, 5 ppm of salt and phenol concentration were used. To avoid sudden change, for every study on salt and phenol, the fluorescence of the QD was measured as a controlled sample, and then all the successive fluorescence with different saline solution and phenol solution was measured. For any measurement the pH of solution was checked first. For Hg2+ detection the pH of solution was fixed at pH 7 and for 4-NP detection the pH of the measuring solution was fixed at pH 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Model fitting of fluorescence quenching data
ce:section.ce:para: Data from the fluorescence quenching spectra were analyzed through pseudo-first-order modelling and other non-linear modellings such as piecewise linear modelling (PWL), exponential, and polynomial modelling. Each data point was taken from the maximum peak height observed from its accorded spectra. The data was studied in normal scale (eqn. 2, Stern-Volmer equation), where Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant as well as converted to logarithmic scale (eqn. 3, 4) where ‘m’ is the slope of linear model and C is the intercept in ordinate axis [65]. X denoted the concentration of analyte in the solution. The data were further observed using PWL, exponential (eqn. 5) and polynomial modelling (eqn. 6), if there was nonlinearity observed in the plotted data points [66,67]. In the case of exponential modelling y0 represents offset, and A1 represents amplitude. For polynomial modelling A, B1 and B2 are the leading coefficients for the corresponding degree of the independent variable.
F = fluorescence of CQDs at different Conc. of the analyte.
F0 = fluorescence of CQDs without the presence of analytes.

Fig. 1 demonstrated the ultra-resolution transmission electron microscopic images of the prepared QDs. It was observed that the metal doping had a significant impact on the particle size distribution of the prepared QD. Native CQD showed to be having a particle size of 5–6 nm (Fig. 1A and B), whereas in case of Mg and Zn doped CQD the particles size reduced, and it was observed to be ranging between 3 and 3.5 nm for MgCQD (Fig. 1C and D) and 4–4.5 nm for ZnCQD (Fig. 1E and F).

All the QDs were found to be almost spherical in shape. In higher scale size, though particle size appeared to be smaller, but when the scale was further fine-tuned and observed through a size distribution of hundred QDs, the CQDs showed an average size of 5.43 nm (Fig. S1A, refer to the supplementary file). MgCQD and ZnCQD showed an average particle size of 3.4 nm (Fig. S1B) and 4.09 nm (Fig. S1C), respectively. Also, the atomic spacing (Fig. S1D and E) demonstrated that ZnCQD was found to have a smaller atomic spacing (0.205 nm) than MgCQD (0.304 nm), which showed that ZnCQD had more compactness in its crystalline structure compared to MgCQD [36]. The lattice spacing of 0.205 nm in ZnCQD demonstrated the presence of both diamond like sp3 carbon (103) and graphite like sp2 carbon (102) [69]. The size of the particle highly depended on the metal doping, as Mg has smaller atomic radius compared to Zn, hence, MgCQD demonstrated smaller size compared to ZnCQD due to its lower lattice strain. Also, the crystallinity depended on the drag force exerted by dopants on the crystalline growth of the particle.

Raman spectra analysis of CQD was performed with 532 nm excitation, as shown in Fig. 2 A. It demonstrated two distinct peaks at 1338 cm−1 (D peak) and 1564 cm−1 (G peak). In case of MgCQD, the G peak shifted and was observed at 1560 cm−1. The G peak was attributed to sp2 hybridization of the graphitic structure of the native CQD present even in doped CQD, whereas the sp3 hybridization represented the D peak which also signifies the amount of defect and functionalization associated with CQD and their doped version.

For the native CQD, the intensity ratio between D and G peaks was found to be 1.1, while due to metallic doping, the ratio was decreased for both MgCQD and ZnCQD (1.005 and 1.09 respectively). The lower D/G ratio indicated that the doping on the native CQDs enhanced the graphitic crystalline structure. Moreover, the complete carbonization and a better crystalline structure induced the excellent photo-stability and PL properties of the doped CQDs.

FTIR spectroscopy was used to analyze the surface ligands present on the CQD. For each CQD (doped or undoped) similar FTIR spectra were observed (Fig. 2B). In case of CQD, The characteristic peak around 3463 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of OH bond from the carboxyl group, which was slightly shifted in case of MgCQD and ZnCQD, and was observed at 3451 and 3463 cm−1, respectively. Peaks at 3070 and 1465 cm−1 were attributed due to the vibration and deformation band of the N–H of the amino functional group, which were again observed at 3052 and 1464 cm−1 for MgCQD and at 3060 and 1461 cm−1 for ZnCQD. Peak observed at 1708 cm−1 for CQD, 1731 cm−1 for MgCQD and at 1721 cm−1 for ZnCQD was due to the stretching vibration of the COO group from carboxyl group. [70] Peaks observed at 1200 and 853 cm−1 for CQD and MgCQD were attributed due to the stretching vibration C-O bond from COOH group. For ZnCQD it was observed at 1211 cm−1 [32].

The optical properties of the QD were further investigated using a fluorescence lifetime study. The fluorescence decay curve of CQD, MgCQD and ZnCQD were fitted perfectly with the triexponential function as given below.

Here, the PL decay function Y(t) is dependent on time (t) and has three different sections of decay lifetime, represented as T1, T2, and T3, which were further represented by their fractional contribution to the PL decay function, represented as A1, A2 and A3, respectively. The decay curve was shown in Fig. 4 and all the details of different decay regions were given in the inset of Fig. 4. The details about all the parameters were given in Table S3. The exponential behavior demonstrated three distinct emissive sites present on all of the prepared CQDs. The T1 represented the direct e-h recombination lifetime, whereas the other two components T2 and T3 demonstrated a longer lifetime which represented the prolonged relaxation of excitons to ground energy levels.

The drop in T2 in MgCQD from 7.29 ns (in CQD) to 6.25 ns was the contribution by Mg doping, whereas a similar case was observed in ZnCQD, where the T2 was decreased to 2.55 ns from 7.29 ns. The QY values were in the agreement with the lifetime of the QDs, where lower QY respective to CQD was due to the higher radiative lifetime and a higher rate of nonradiative decay. This fact might be due to the trapping of excitons on the surface defects present on the CQD lattice. The reduced lifetime in metallophilic doped CQD (MgCQD and ZnCQD) revealed the improved mobility of excitons in the lattice structure of MgCQD and ZnCQD.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed for ZnCQD to determine the elemental composition and understand the surface functionalization groups. Fig. 5 A demonstrated the elemental survey scan of ZnCQD and confirmed the presence of C, N, O and Zn elements on its surface. Peaks below 200 eV in Fig. 5A were due to the Si substrate used for the XPS analysis. The atomic concentration level was mentioned in the inset table of Fig. 5A, which clearly showed the presence of Zn (2.37 %) in the atomic structure of ZnCQD, thus, confirming the doping of Zn in ZnCQD. Moreover, it demonstrated the presence of N atom as well; however, the percentage (5.33 %) seemed less due to the high-temperature carbonization. The intensity peaks corresponding to C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s and Zn 2p were observed at 280, 390, 529, and 1020 eV, respectively. The core elemental peaks were further deconvoluted to find the functional groups expressed on the surface of ZnCQD. Fig. 5B demonstrated the deconvoluted peaks of C 1 s. The observed peaks at 281, 283, 285 and 287 eV corresponded to the functional groups of C–C, C-O, C = O and C-N, respectively [71]. The N 1 s peak intensity was found to be very low, however when zoomed in and deconvoluted N-C and N–H bond peaks were observed at 397 eV and 404 eV, respectively (Fig. 5C). The decreased peak height of N 1 s in the elemental scan might be due to the high-temperature carbonization which caused the breaking of amine bonds present in the ESM structure. Fig. 5D demonstrated the deconvoluted peaks of O 1 s, and showed that the peaks at 529.4 eV and 530 eV were due to the functional groups of O-C and O = C. The peak of Zn 2p was also deconvoluted and represented in Fig. 5E. After deconvolution two major peaks were observed at 1020 eV and 1040 eV, which was due to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 [72].

XPS analysis was performed for MgCQD as well to investigate its elemental composition. Fig. S4A demonstrated the elemental survey scan of MgCQD and confirmed the presence of C, N, O and Mg elements on its surface. However, the peaks generated for Mg 2 s was found to be very small and atomic percentage of Mg was calculated to be 4%. The core elemental peaks related to MgCQD were further deconvoluted for better understand of the functional groups available on its surface. Fig. S4B demonstrated the deconvoluted peaks of C 1 s present in MgCQD. The functional groups of C–C, C-O, C = O and C-N were observed at 282, 283, 286 and 287 eV, respectively. The deconvoluted N 1 s sepctra revealed that N-C and N–H bond peaks were observed at 396 eV and 404 eV, respectively (Fig. S4C). Peaks due to functional groups of O-C and O = C were observed at 529 eV and 532 eV (Fig. S4D), respectively, as found from deconvoluted O 1 s spectra. Denconvoluted Mg 2 s spectra confirmed the presence of Mg 2 s core in MgCQD. The major functional groups of Mg-O and Mg 2 s were observed at 82.34 eV and 87 eV, respectively (Fig. S4E).

The fluorescence of the prepared QDs was highly dependent on pH as shown in Fig. 6 . The study was performed in the pH range of 2 to 12. It was observed that in the case of MgCQD, the pH-dependent fluorescence change was minimal, however, in the case of ZnCQD, the fluorescence enhanced significantly in alkaline pH. In the case of MgCQD, the fluorescence intensity was increased by14% at pH 12, compared to pH 7 (Fig. 6A), whereas for ZnCQD, the intensity was increased by 87% (Fig. 6B). At lower pH, the fluorescence reduction was due to the presence of high H+ ions, as the surface got protonated. Therefore, the excited electrons found a path for non-radiative recombination and resulted in fluorescence reduction.

It was also observed that at higher pH, the emission peak was blue shifted for both the QDs, however, the intensity was suddenly increased after pH 9. The reason might be due to the change of –COOH to –COO− and –OH groups to –O−. This fact promoted the formation of delocalized π bonds and increased n electrons which subsequently enhanced the fluorescence. Hence, the change in the fluorescence in both of the QDs was mainly ascribed to the protonation and deprotonation of the surface groups. Therefore, the fluorescence of QDs was highly affected by the pH of the solution (Fig. 6C) [73].

The PL change at different pH was further analyzed through zeta potential measurement (ζ). The ζ value for both the MgCQD and ZnCQD was found to be negative in the alkaline pH medium (-36 mV and −34.5 mV respectively). The ζ value was found to shift towards positive side as the pH was decreased, and it was due to the protonation of the available sites present on the QD surface (Fig. S5). The zeta potential dropped to −1.67 mV for MgCQD and −0.145 mV for ZnCQD in acidic pH condition. The fluorescence drop in acidic pH was believed to be due to the photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and the interaction between lone pairs of electrons available in different nitrogen sites as well as metallophilic doped sites. Thus, it was revealed from the zeta potential analysis that the fluorescence of the QD was highly sensitive to the pH of the environment. Further, we studied the selectivity towards metal ions by MgCQD and ZnCQD at acidic pH (pH 2), neutral pH (pH 7) and alkaline pH (pH 9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity toward metal ions in different pH by MgCQD and ZnCQD
ce:section.ce:para: Here, we demonstrated the interaction of MgCQD and ZnCQD with different metal ions (5 ppm) at different pH, to confirm their selectivity. It was observed that at pH 2, none of the QDs showed any selectivity towards metal ions (Fig. 7 A and Fig. 8 A), however, multiple metal ion selectivity was observed at pH 9. In neutral pH (pH 7), both the QDs showed sensitivity towards mercury ion with 5 ppm of concentration, where the PL of MgCQD was observed to decrease by 30% and for ZnCQD by 40% (Fig. 7B and Fig. 8B).

As mentioned earlier, however, in the alkaline pH medium, MgCQD showed sensitivity towards Co2+, Hg2+, Mg2+ and Ni2+ (Fig. 7C) with quenching in the fluorescence by 18%, 27%, 11% and 12%, respectively (Fig. 7D). For ZnCQD, the selectivity was observed towards Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+ and Ni2+ with PL quenching of 11%, 20%, 18%, 32% and 19%, respectively (Fig. 8C and D).

The main reason for non-selectivity in acidic pH was due to the protonation of surface trap sites and functionalized groups, which restrained the selectivity, and hence, restricted the nonradiative pathway for excited electrons. Further, at higher pH, due to the deprotonation and the formation of –COO− and –O− groups on the surface of the QD, increased availability of surface ligands and hence, the selectivity towards different metal ions were observed. It established that both the QDs have high sensitivity towards Hg2+ ions in neutral pH and multiple metal ion selectivity at alkaline pH. Hence, we further tested the sensitivity towards mercury ions in neutral pH.

The sensitivity of the QDs towards Hg2+ ions at pH 7 with an extended concentration range (0–20 ppm) was also investigated (Fig. 9 A). The PL of the MgCQD was observed to be gradually quenched as the concentration of Hg2+ increased. With a concentration of 20 ppm Hg2+, the PL intensity was quenched at nearly 70%. The interaction of QDs with other metal ions at 20 ppm was also studied. It was observed that at 20 ppm, QD fluorescence quenching was observed in the presence of Fe3+ and Cu2+ (Fig. 9B). The presence of Fe3+ produced a PL quenching of MgCQD of around 20% and Cu2+ produced around 35% quenching (Fig. S6A). To eliminate the effect of Fe3+ and Cu2+ quenching, masking agents, such as NaF and Na4P2O7 were used [74]. The addition of a masking agent helped to recover the PL of MgCQD by more than 95% of the original PL value in the presence of Fe3+ (Fig. 9C) and Cu2+ (Fig. 9D). This combination of MgCQD with masking agent was further used for selective sensing of Hg2+ in the presence of other metal ions to nullify the quenching effect contributed by Fe3+ and Cu2+ (Fig. S6B). It was further observed that the use of the masking agent did not affect the quenching pattern of MgCQD in presence of Hg2+ and other metal ions (Fig. S6C).

The kinetics of the sensing was also analyzed using different mathematical models. Initially, the whole data region (0–20 ppm) was fitted using a PWL model and a nonlinear model, as the data points were observed to be non-linear. Further, the same model was used using semi-logarithmic data points to enhance the accuracy. For the first case (PWL), three linear regions were observed with R2 value of 0.990, and for the non-linear modelling, a polynomial model with degree 2 was used (Fig. S7A), which showed R2 value of 0.993 (Table S4). Further, in a semi-logarithmic scale, the above said model showed an accuracy of R2 value of 0.997 and 0.985, respectively (Fig. S7B). The details of the slope, intercept and breaking points were mentioned in Table S4.

In the PWL modeling, it was observed that there were multiple linear regions, so the full data range was divided into two data regions where the PL quenching showed high linearity, in 0–6 ppm and 12–20 ppm. In the first case, for the normal data points the PL data showed a great linearity with a R2 value of 0.999 and a slope of 0.0969 min−1. However, for semi-logarithmic data points, it demonstrated R2 value of 0.995 and slope of 0.07906 min−1 (Fig. S7C). For the second linear region, i.e. 12–20 ppm, for normal data points, it showed a slope of 0.098 min−1(Fig. S7D), and for semi-logarithmic data points, it showed a slope of 0.074 min−1 (Table S5). In case of the whole data range, both polynomial model and PWL kinetic model demonstrated an accurate fit with a R2 value of 0.99.

We also investigated the sensitivity towards Hg2+ ion at pH 7 with a concentration range of 0–20 ppm using ZnCQD as well (Fig. 10 A). The PL intensity was found to be quenched by 52% at a concentration of 20 ppm Hg2+, which was lower compared to MgCQD (70%). The PL quenching of ZnCQD was further evaluated in the presence of other metal ions as well. It was observed that at 20 ppm, there was a quenching in the presence of Fe3+ only (Fig. 10B). In the presence of Fe3+, the PL produced by ZnCQD was quenched by 22% (Fig. S8A). Hence, to reduce the PL quenching by Fe3+, NaF was used as a masking agent, and the PL of ZnCQD was recovered to its original value (Fig. 10C). ZnCQD with masking agent for Fe3+ was further used for a sensing of Hg2+ in the presence of different metal ions (Fig. 10D). It was observed that after the use of masking agent, the PL quenching by Hg2+ in the presence of other metal ions, especially Fe3+, was quite similar (Fig. S8B).

The kinetics of the sensing data was also analyzed using different mathematical models. Initially, the whole data range (0–20 ppm) was fitted using a PWL model and a nonlinear model (exponential model), as the data points showed to be highly non-linear (Fig. S9A). Further, the same models were used on semi-logarithmic data points to improve the accuracy of the fitting (Fig. S9B). The accuracy of the fitting of different models with respect to the data points is provided in Table S6. In case of PWL modeling, two linear regions were observed, so the whole data region (0–20 ppm) was divided into two, i.e. 0–6 ppm (Fig. S9C) and 8–20 ppm (Fig. S9D), where the mathematical model was highly linear. For both the region, linear modelling was performed based on normal data points and their corresponding semi-logarithmic data points. The details of the slope and accuracy of the fitted model is given in (Table S7). Exponential model demonstrated a R2 fit of 0.986 for whole data set of Hg2+ detection using ZnCQD, whereas PWL model showed a R2 fit of 0.991 for the same data range. Hence, for ZnCQD as Hg2+ sensing, PWL model followed the whole data trend more accurately.

As it was observed that in the acidic pH environment, the synthesized QDs showed very low sensitivity towards present metal ions, we further investigated their use in the detection of different phenol derivatives, such as phenol, 2-nitrophenol (2-NP), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), di- nitrophenol (DNP) and tri- nitrophenol (TNP), as well. Here, we tested our prepared QD in 0–20 ppm of different phenol derivatives, and it was observed that in the presence of only 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) the QDs exhibited substantial fluorescence quenching. It was observed that for both MgCQD and ZnCQD, 20 ppm of 4-NP reduced the PL intensity by more than 90% (Fig. 11 A and Fig. 11B). Further, when the selectivity of the QDs, in the presence of phenol derivatives and metal ions was assessed, no metal ion was found to quench the PL to the extent as quenched by 4-NP (Fig. 11C and Fig. 11D). In case of other phenol derivatives, only DNP caused a slight fluorescence quenching of the QDs, approximately by 18%.

However, it was also observed that in the simultaneous presence of 20 ppm of 4-NP and other different metal and phenol derivatives, the fluorescence quenching pattern was highly similar, which demonstrated a high selectivity of 4-NP by both MgCQD and ZnCQD in acidic pH (Fig. S10) even when other metals and phenol derivatives were present. Hence, it was proved that the prepared nanomaterial was highly sensitive to 4-NP at pH 2. Further, the kinetics was assessed using only linear modeling, as the data points appeared to be very linear [84].

It was observed that for MgCQD, I0/I plot followed the linear trend very accurately with a slope of 0.22095 min−1 and R2 value of 0.990 (Table S8). However, the accuracy of the model was found reduced when it was applied to semi-logarithmic data (Fig. S11A). For semi-logarithmic data points, the model followed a very accurate linear trend in the 4–20 ppm region (Fig. S11A (inset)). Further, in case of ZnCQD, the model was very linear for both of the data points (I0/I and ln (I0/I)) with R2 value of more than 0.99 for both of the cases (Fig. S11B).

Recent literature on Hg2+ and 4-NP sensing using CQD-based fluorescent sensing systems were reported in Table 2 and Table 3 , respectively, which were compared based on their source of synthesis, detection range and synthesis procedure. It revealed that our prepared probes i.e. MgCQD and ZnCQD might be highly efficient for pH-dependent dual sensing of Hg2+ and 4-NP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 4-NP detection at neutral pH by ZnCQD
ce:section.ce:para: Here, we also investigated the interaction of ZnCQD with 4-NP at pH 7 to isolate different sensing strategies for Hg2+ and 4-NP. It was observed that ZnCQD possessed a substantial level of quenching in the presence of 4-NP at neutral pH as well (demonstrating 90% PL quenching, Fig. S12A), and the quenching was highly linear as well in nature (Fig. S12B). The kinetic of the quenching showed a PL reduction slope of −0.1138 ppm−1 and R2 of 0.996.

Hence, it was observed that our prepared metal doped QDs showed sensitivity to both 4-NP and Hg2+ at pH 7, but to only 4-NP at pH 2. The fluorescence quenching relation with respect to 4-NP concentration at both the pH (pH 2 and 7) was highly linear (Fig. S11 and Fig. S12). Also, in case of mercury, the fluorescence quenching relation with respect to its concentration at pH 7 was highly linear in nature (Fig. S7 and Fig. S9) as well. Moreover, although the sensing agent was very sensitive to both the molecules, it could not be used for the simultaneous detection of both 4-NP and Hg2+ in the same solution. It could detect 4-NP in any solution, but could detect Hg2+ in which there was no trace of 4-NP. Thus, during any testing, it was preferred to always first measure the concentration of 4-NP at pH 2. As at pH 2 there was no interaction of mercury on the detection of 4-NP, the actual concentration of 4-NP could easily be calculated. Further, for Hg2+ detection, once it was confirmed that the solution contains no 4-NP, then at pH 7 the concentration of Hg2+ could be calculated. Hence, to confirm whether the testing water sample contained Hg2+ or 4-NP, we presented a truth table for its determination (Table S9). Also, there corresponding truth table for selection of Hg2+ or 4-NP at pH 7 and pH 2 were represented in Table S10 and Table S11, respectively. Furthermore, there corresponding logic gate implications were also mentioned [83].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism for Hg2+ and 4-NP detection
ce:section.ce:para: One possible mechanism for fluorescence quenching could be the inner filter effect where the excitation or emission spectrum of the QD matches with the absorbance spectra of the analyte, due to which, QD present in the solution does not get strong excitation, and hence a fluorescence quenching is observed [94]. Another reason could be due to the aggregation of the QD via the disruption in the capping layer.

Other possibilities included the replacement of core atoms by analyte or by electron transfer through nonradiative recombination of the excitons by the analyte attachment through different trap sites [95]. For Hg2+ sensing, the mechanism was highly dependent on dynamic quenching and static quenching. The formation of a complex in the presence of Hg2+ provoked nonradiative charge transfer and agglomeration of the QDs. However, in the case of 4-NP (in acidic pH), most of the trap sites and ligands were protonated due to the presence of a high concentration of H+ ion, and hence, it was found that the quenching was due to the inner filter effect between the absorbance spectra of 4-NP colliding with the absorbance spectra of metallophilic doped CQD (MgCQD and ZnCQD).

FTIR spectra were used to investigate the complex creation or if there was any static quenching. It was observed that in the case of MgCQD after sensing of Hg2+ and 4-NP, the FTIR spectra showed high discrepancy compared to fresh MgCQD (Fig. S13A). There was a big shift in the absorbance peak of OH stretching at 3420 cm−1, which indicated that there was an interaction between available ligands on MgCQD surface and Hg2+. However, in the case of ZnCQD and Hg2+, no observable peak shift was witnessed in any of the ligand stretching vibrations (Fig. S13B).

Similar evidence was observed in their kinetic plot as well. During Hg2+ detection by MgCQD, two linear regions were observed (Fig. S7A), as the interaction between QD and Hg2+ ion was mainly based on surface based attachment, both static and dynamic quenching played their part. In the first PWL linear zone, the attachment of ions were very fast, as all the surface ligands in the QD were free for attachment, and thus the corresponding fluorescence quenching was dynamic in nature.

However, after a certain period of time, during the second linear region of PWL plot, as the number of Hg2+ ion started to get much higher, and most of the surface sites were already occupied, the MgCQDs slowly started to agglomerate, and their overall physical and optical property changed, due to which the rate of fluorescence quenching in that region demonstrated to be higher due to the introduction of static quenching. Whereas, in case of ZnCQD, as very low static quenching was observed, the second linear region demonstrated slower fluorescence quenching rate than the first linear region (Fig. S9A). The reason was solely because with time most of the available surface sites were occupied, and slower rate of ion attachment was observed (during the second linear region), which directly affected in the slower rate of fluorescence quenching in ZnCQD.

For 4-NP detection, there was no evidence of FTIR peak change corresponding to MgCQD and ZnCQD (Fig. S13A and S12B respectively), clearly indicating that there was no static quenching or complex creation for QDs in the presence of 4-NP. Further, fluorescence lifetime decay spectra of both QDs also confirmed that there was an ignorable lifetime change (Fig. 12) in the presence of 4-NP. The T2 value changed from 6.25 ns to 6.85 ns for MgCQD and T3 was changed from 6.77 ns to 6.00 ns for ZnCQD (Table S12). Hence, in the presence of 4-NP, it was confirmed that dynamic quenching was not a crucial part in case of MgCQD and ZnCQD. Hence, the quenching was not completed due to the non-radiative recombination of excitons present on the conduction band of the QD.

However, in another way it was also observed that 4-NP showed to have an absorbance range of 320––340 nm which merges with the absorbance range of the prepared metal doped CQDs (Fig. 13 ), thus the inner filter effect must have affected the QD fluorescence as well. The excitation energy of the QD (excited at the wavelength of 330 nm for MgCQD, 320 nm for ZnCQD) was absorbed beforehand by the already present 4-NP molecules in the solution, which affected the creation of excitons in the QD and thus, the quenching of the fluorescence was observed [84].

Here, we successfully synthesized metal doped CQDs from hen feathers, which were highly fluorescent, and had a spherical size of ∼ 4 nm. The interaction between synthesized QDs and metal ions in neutral pH confirmed that the prepared QDs were highly sensitive to mercury ions. It was also concluded that among prepared metal doped (Mg, Zn) QDs, Zn doped CQDs (ZnCQD) showed the lowest band gap energy and highest sensitivity towards mercury ions in neutral pH. The sensing potential of our prepared QDs was pH specific and highly fluorescent at pH 12. Moreover, due to the protonation, the QDs showed no fluorescence quenching at pH 2 in the presence of metal ions, but it sensed 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) due to the inner filter effect mechanism. MgCQD confirmed linearity between 0 and 6 ppm and 12–20 ppm for the detection of mercury ions, whereas, in the case of ZnCQD the linearity was found to be within 0–6 ppm and 8–20 ppm. Also, for the overall range of 0–20 ppm, among various mathematicacl models (PWL, Polynomial, and exponential), PWL kinetic model accurately followed the whole data trend for Hg2+ sensing by MgCQD and ZnCQD. For 4-NP sensing, better linearity was found in the region of 0–20 ppm by both MgCQD and ZnCQD. The quenching mechanism for Hg2+ was found to be mainly dynamic quenching for both MgCQD and ZnCQD, while for 4-NP, the quenching was primarily due to the inner filter effect for both MgCQD and ZnCQD. From the results, ZnCQD showed to be a better performer due to its low band gap, high quantum yield, good pH sensitivity, and high selectivity toward mercury as well as 4-NP. Also, as the sensing of 4-NP was observed in pH 7 by ZnCQD as well, hence, the sensing agent although is sensitive to both Hg2+ and 4-NP, can only detect one of them in a single test case. Therefore, our study concluded that hen feathers can be effectively recycled and converted into a valuable nanomaterial product such as Mg and Zn doped carbon quantum dots for developing pH-dependent fluorescence-based dual sensing methods for Hg2+ and 4-Nitrophenol, which might be highly useful in the detection of pollutants and further their remediation.

Sohel Das: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Uma Sankar Mondal: Manuscript Preparation. Subhankar Paul: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.
